text: The limits of conscious perception are not failures of evolution — they are features. Temporal masking, the attentional blink, the refractory period: each reflects a design in which consolidation takes priority over throughput. A system that tried to consciously register every stimulus would be overwhelmed; better to process one thing deeply than two things poorly. Anne Treisman's feature integration theory showed that conscious perception requires binding — assembling separate features like color, shape, and motion into a unified object — and binding demands focused attention. The bottleneck is the price of coherence. What we call the limits of mind are, in another light, the conditions that make a unified experience of the world possible.
question: According to Treisman's feature integration theory, why does conscious perception require focused attention?

options.text: To suppress irrelevant stimuli that compete for the same sensory channel
To increase the speed at which the brain samples the visual field
To perform binding — assembling separate features into a unified object
To transfer information from working memory into long-term storage
